title: Seeing the forbidden: overcoming optical selection rules through nanophotonic integration
authors: Alex H. Rubin, Vytautas Žalandauskas, Pranta Saha, Rubek Poudel, Aurora Teien, Liam Hofmann, Nathan R. Gonzalez, Scott Dhuey, Marianne Etzelmüller Bathen, Marina Radulaski
abstract: Optically addressable spin defects in silicon carbide, including the neutral divacancy (VV$^0$) and the negative nitrogen-vacancy (NV$^-$), are among leading building blocks of solid-state quantum technologies. Integrating these defects into photonic structures such as nanopillars improves photon collection efficiency, but the consequences extend further. We show that the sub-wavelength geometry of nanopillars drastically modifies the local electromagnetic environment, providing optical access to defect transitions that are otherwise suppressed by selection rules in bulk material. Using low-temperature photoluminescence spectroscopy, we observe that emission from the PL3 divacancy, which is nearly absent in planar devices, becomes pronounced in nanopillars owing to a polarization transformation of the excitation field within the pillar. We further leverage the orientation-dependent collection of nanopillars to resolve the origin of previously ambiguous spectral lines. In particular, the NV4$'$ feature displays the signal enhancement expected for axially oriented NV$^-$ centres, consistent with assignment to a higher excited state of the $kh$ defect configuration. Our results establish nanophotonic integration as a symmetry-sensitive probe that can both activate nominally dark transitions and identify the dipole character of poorly understood defect states.
summary: The paper demonstrates that integrating silicon carbide defects into nanopillars can overcome optical selection rules by generating a longitudinal electric field component. This allows researchers to observe 'dark' transitions and use nanophotonic structures as a symmetry-sensitive tool to identify the electronic structure of quantum defects. This approach provides a new way to characterize and manipulate spin defects for quantum technologies.
main_problem: The study addresses how optical selection rules in bulk 4H-silicon carbide suppress certain defect transitions and seeks to use nanophotonic integration to overcome these rules and resolve the identity of ambiguous spectral lines.
main_result: Nanopillars act as polarization converters that generate a longitudinal electric field component, thereby 'unlocking' previously dark transitions like the PL3 divacancy and allowing for the symmetry-based identification of defect states.
method: The researchers combined low-temperature photoluminescence spectroscopy and temperature-dependent measurements with FDTD simulations and first-principles DFT calculations using the r2SCAN functional.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of 4H-silicon carbide (4H-SiC) containing optically addressable spin defects, specifically neutral divacancies (VV0) and nitrogen-vacancy (NV-) centers, integrated into sub-wavelength dielectric nanopillars.
key_observables: Photoluminescence (PL) spectra, polarization-dependent emission, signal enhancement factors, and thermal quenching/activation energies.
important_parameters: Excitation wavelength (785 nm and 852 nm), temperature range (1.6 K to 300 K), nanopillar diameters (300 nm to 1100 nm), and crystallographic configurations (axial vs. basal).
main_assumptions: The nanopillars were primarily optimized for photon collection efficiency rather than specific selection-rule engineering, and the $E_z$ component is treated as an incidental byproduct of the geometry.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows SEM images and PL maps of nanopillars; Figure 2 and 3 compare PL spectra and transition dipole moments between bulk and nanopillar geometries; Figure 4 and supplementary figures show temperature-dependent PL and Arrhenius plots for extracting activation energies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of selection rules, describes the nanophotonic platform and experimental/computational methods, presents the activation of dark transitions via polarization transformation, uses symmetry analysis to identify specific defect states, and concludes with the utility of nanophotonics as a spectroscopic tool.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information because it demonstrates a method to control light-matter interaction at the nanoscale, enabling access to previously inaccessible quantum emitters and providing a tool for characterizing the symmetry of solid-state qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The scale bar represents 20 µm. b 2D photoluminescence map of an array of 600 nm diameter nanopillars taken via scanning confocal microscopy using 785 nm excitation and SNSPD detection. c, d Examples of photoluminescence spectra of VV0 and NV−defects taken from 1100 nm diameter nanopillars and from unetched bulk areas of the sample.
